Caption: Cold-plate configurations: (a) top, (b) bottom, (c) top and bottom. Plates reduce Tmax relative to air cooling and improve uniformity at contact faces, but the pack centre remains comparatively warm due to limited conduction pathways.

Single-phase immersion cooling with 3 M™ Novec™ 7100 at (a,c) without and (b,d) with porous pads; room-temperature inlet (a,b) and Tin = 32∘C (c,d). Porous media enhance uniformity but can raise surface temperature due to added conduction resistance.

Two-phase immersion with 3 M™ Novec™ 7000 at T i n ≈ 31 - 32 ° C : (a) without and (b) with porous pads; (c) comparison of T m a x ( t ) . The porous case maintains near-isothermal behaviour with T m a x ≤ 36.8 ° C and Δ T ≤ 2.3 K at 1C.

Early-stage bubble nucleation and motion in two-phase immersion (schematic right: with/without porous guidance). Measured departure diameters d d e p ≈ 100 - 200 μ m and event rates from high-speed clips (Sec. 3.4) map via Eq. (13) to a latent-heat flux within 20–30 % of the electrical heat flux, corroborating the pack-level capping of T m a x and low Δ T in Fig. 10.
Interstitial velocity fields in porous media at ε = 0.6 vs ε = 0.8 . Higher porosity yields more uniform velocities and fewer stagnant pockets, aiding liquid supply and vapour venting.

Vapour volume fraction fields with and without porous media. Porous pads act as liquid feeders and vapour chimneys, reducing vapour-fraction variance and shortening rewetting times; this stabilises nucleate boiling and improves uniformity.

Cross-method comparison at 1C: heat-removal ratio Q out / Q in (left axis) and normalised non-uniformity Δ T max / T in (right axis). Two-phase immersion with porous media delivers the best combination of low T max , low Δ T , and high removal ratio.
